moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the English Opening, one of the most flexible opening systems in chess. By playing c4 instead of e4, we control the d5 square from the side and aim to build a long-term positional advantage rather than seek immediate tactical clashes. This move prepares to expand on the queenside and kingside while keeping our position adaptable to Black's setup.

Black plays an extremely aggressive and unusual move, immediately attacking our c1-h6 diagonal and signaling an intention to launch a kingside attack with a fianchettoed bishop. This is the Zilbermints Gambit—Black sacrifices pawn structure and safety to generate immediate counterplay. While this move looks dangerous, it severely weakens Black's king position, and we should meet it with solid central play rather than panic.

We immediately strike in the center, claiming space and opening lines for our pieces while Black's kingside is overextended. By pushing d4, we control the critical e5 and c5 squares, and we prepare to develop naturally while Black must justify the risky pawn push. This move also prevents Black from easily playing ...e6 to solidify the center, forcing Black into immediate decisions.

Black supports the advanced g-pawn and fights for central space, but this further weakens the kingside, especially around the f6 and h6 squares. Black is essentially betting on rapid attacking chances to compensate for a severely compromised king safety. We should now focus on sound development, proper piece placement (especially bringing our knight to f3 and bishop to active squares), and keeping our king safe while Black's position becomes increasingly vulnerable to tactical blows.
